pattern: Deleting every copy of one value is the same as replacing every copy of it with a free zero bridge, so the best subarray after the operation is just a Kadane-style merge of the untouched runs between those bridges -- and only negative candidate values are ever worth trying.
hints: Removing all copies of x turns sum(l..r) - x*count_x(l..r) into the sum of the same range with every x swapped for 0, so only negative x can ever help (positive or zero x never improves things).

For a fixed x, the array splits into runs separated by x's occurrences; treat each x as a free zero and merge the runs left to right the way a segment tree merges Kadane blocks (track each block's total sum, best prefix, best suffix, and best subarray).

Precompute a Kadane-merge segment tree once for range queries, then for every distinct negative value x, stitch together the runs between its occurrences (with zero blocks in between) and take the merged best subarray as that value's candidate answer; the overall answer is the best candidate over all x plus the untouched baseline.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, removing a middling negative helps most
all non-negative, no removal ever helps
removing the only negative value would empty the array, so it must be skipped
a very negative single spike separates two positive runs
all negative, best is a single least-negative element
removing a repeated small negative beats removing a rarer larger one

stress.description: long random array with a healthy mix of repeated negative values
